---
author: jshn9515
date: 2026-05-23
date-modified: 2026-05-23
open-graph:
  title: ch2.3-dataset-and-dataloader
  description: "2.3 Data Loading in PyTorch: Dataset, DataLoader, and Batching"
title: "2.3 Data Loading in PyTorch: Dataset, DataLoader, and Batching"
order: 3
---

A complete training process needs more than a model and gradients: it also needs a data pipeline.

PyTorch separates this pipeline into `Dataset`, which defines individual samples, and `DataLoader`, which batches, shuffles, collates, and optionally prefetches those samples.

The examples start with tensor data and then move toward the practical issues that appear in real training scripts.

This section develops the concept step by step and uses the following code to make the behavior concrete.

```python
X = torch.randn(1000, 10)
y = torch.randn(1000, 1)
```

The important point is the distinction between mathematical intent and the way PyTorch records or manages state.

```python
batch_size = 32

X_batch = X[:batch_size]
y_batch = y[:batch_size]
```

In normal training code, this pattern keeps the model logic explicit while letting PyTorch handle the repetitive bookkeeping.

```{python}
from collections.abc import Iterator

import torch
import torch.utils.data as utils
from torch import Tensor

print('PyTorch version:', torch.__version__)
```

```{python}
device = torch.accelerator.current_accelerator(check_available=True)
if device is None:
    device = torch.device('cpu')
print('Using device:', device)
```

## 2.3.1 Dataset: Accessing Samples Through a Unified Interface

The example below is small on purpose: it exposes the mechanism without burying it inside a large model.

```text
dataset -> mini-batch -> model -> loss -> backward -> optimizer step
```

These details are easy to overlook, but they explain many common PyTorch errors and performance surprises.

```text
cat_001.jpg -> label 0
dog_001.jpg -> label 1
cat_002.jpg -> label 0
...
```

This section develops the concept step by step and uses the following code to make the behavior concrete.

```python
sample = dataset[index]
```

The important point is the distinction between mathematical intent and the way PyTorch records or manages state.

```{python}
class SimpleTensorDataset(utils.Dataset):
    def __init__(self, X: Tensor, y: Tensor):
        if len(X) != len(y):
            raise ValueError('X and y must have the same length.')
        self.X = X
        self.y = y

    def __len__(self) -> int:
        return len(self.X)

    def __getitem__(self, index: int) -> tuple[Tensor, Tensor]:
        X = self.X[index]
        y = self.y[index]
        return X, y
```

In normal training code, this pattern keeps the model logic explicit while letting PyTorch handle the repetitive bookkeeping.

```{python}
X = torch.randn(1000, 10)
y = torch.randn(1000, 1)

dataset = SimpleTensorDataset(X, y)
x0, y0 = dataset[0]

print('Dataset length:', len(dataset))
print('First input shape:', x0.shape)
print('First target shape:', y0.shape)
```

The example below is small on purpose: it exposes the mechanism without burying it inside a large model.

```{python}
dataset = utils.TensorDataset(X, y)
x0, y0 = dataset[0]

print('First input shape:', x0.shape)
print('First target shape:', y0.shape)
```

These details are easy to overlook, but they explain many common PyTorch errors and performance surprises.

## 2.3.2 DataLoader: Organizing Samples into Mini-Batches

This section develops the concept step by step and uses the following code to make the behavior concrete.

```{python}
dataloader = utils.DataLoader(
    dataset,
    batch_size=32,
    shuffle=True,
)

X, y = next(iter(dataloader))
print('Input batch shape:', X.shape)
print('Target batch shape:', y.shape)
```

The important point is the distinction between mathematical intent and the way PyTorch records or manages state.

```python
for X, y in dataloader:
    y_pred = model(X)
    loss = loss_fn(y_pred, y)
    loss.backward()
```

In normal training code, this pattern keeps the model logic explicit while letting PyTorch handle the repetitive bookkeeping.

```text
Dataset:    index -> sample
DataLoader: samples -> batch
```

The example below is small on purpose: it exposes the mechanism without burying it inside a large model.

## 2.3.3 collate_fn: How Samples Are Packed into a Batch

These details are easy to overlook, but they explain many common PyTorch errors and performance surprises.

```text
x: (32, 10)
y: (32, 1)
```

This section develops the concept step by step and uses the following code to make the behavior concrete.

```{python}
class VariableLengthDataset(utils.Dataset):
    def __init__(self):
        self.samples = [
            torch.tensor([1, 2, 3]),
            torch.tensor([4, 5]),
            torch.tensor([6, 7, 8, 9]),
            torch.tensor([10]),
        ]

    def __len__(self) -> int:
        return len(self.samples)

    def __getitem__(self, index: int) -> Tensor:
        return self.samples[index]
```

The important point is the distinction between mathematical intent and the way PyTorch records or manages state.

```{python}
dataset = VariableLengthDataset()
dataloader = utils.DataLoader(dataset, batch_size=2)

try:
    batch = next(iter(dataloader))
except RuntimeError as err:
    print('RuntimeError:', err)
```

In normal training code, this pattern keeps the model logic explicit while letting PyTorch handle the repetitive bookkeeping.

```{python}
def pad_collate_fn(batch: list[Tensor]) -> tuple[Tensor, Tensor]:
    lengths = torch.tensor([len(x) for x in batch])
    max_len = lengths.max().item()

    padded = torch.zeros(len(batch), max_len, dtype=torch.long)
    for i, x in enumerate(batch):
        padded[i, : len(x)] = x

    return padded, lengths
```

The example below is small on purpose: it exposes the mechanism without burying it inside a large model.

```{python}
dataloader = utils.DataLoader(
    dataset,
    batch_size=2,
    collate_fn=pad_collate_fn,
)

for tokens, lengths in dataloader:
    print(f'tokens:\n{tokens}')
    print(f'lengths: {lengths}\n')
```

These details are easy to overlook, but they explain many common PyTorch errors and performance surprises.

## 2.3.4 num_workers: Parallelizing Data Loading and Model Computation

This section develops the concept step by step and uses the following code to make the behavior concrete.

```text
read batch -> train one step -> read batch -> train one step -> ...
```

The important point is the distinction between mathematical intent and the way PyTorch records or manages state.

```{python}
dataloader = utils.DataLoader(
    dataset,
    batch_size=32,
    shuffle=True,
    num_workers=0,
)
```

In normal training code, this pattern keeps the model logic explicit while letting PyTorch handle the repetitive bookkeeping.

```text
worker 0 -> prepare batch
worker 1 -> prepare batch
worker 2 -> prepare batch
main process -> train model
```

The example below is small on purpose: it exposes the mechanism without burying it inside a large model.

```python
if __name__ == '__main__':
    main()
```

These details are easy to overlook, but they explain many common PyTorch errors and performance surprises.

::: {.callout-warning}

:::

## 2.3.5 persistent_workers: Avoid Restarting Workers Every Epoch

This section develops the concept step by step and uses the following code to make the behavior concrete.

```{python}
dataloader = utils.DataLoader(
    dataset,
    batch_size=32,
    shuffle=True,
    num_workers=2,
    persistent_workers=True,
)
```

::: {.callout-warning}

The important point is the distinction between mathematical intent and the way PyTorch records or manages state.

:::

## 2.3.6 pin_memory: Whether to Put Batches in Page-Locked Memory

::: {.callout-tip}

In normal training code, this pattern keeps the model logic explicit while letting PyTorch handle the repetitive bookkeeping.

:::

```python
X = X.to(device)
y = y.to(device)
```

The example below is small on purpose: it exposes the mechanism without burying it inside a large model.

```{python}
dataloader = utils.DataLoader(
    dataset,
    batch_size=32,
    shuffle=True,
    pin_memory=device.type == 'cuda',
)
```

These details are easy to overlook, but they explain many common PyTorch errors and performance surprises.

```python
pin_memory=True
```

This section develops the concept step by step and uses the following code to make the behavior concrete.

```python
X = X.to('cuda', non_blocking=True)
y = y.to('cuda', non_blocking=True)
```

The important point is the distinction between mathematical intent and the way PyTorch records or manages state.

## 2.3.7 IterableDataset: When Data Cannot Be Randomly Accessed

In normal training code, this pattern keeps the model logic explicit while letting PyTorch handle the repetitive bookkeeping.

```python
sample = dataset[index]
```

The example below is small on purpose: it exposes the mechanism without burying it inside a large model.

```text
sample_1 -> sample_2 -> sample_3 -> ...
```

These details are easy to overlook, but they explain many common PyTorch errors and performance surprises.

```{python}
class SimpleCountingDataset(utils.IterableDataset):
    def __init__(self, end: int):
        self.end = end

    def __iter__(self) -> Iterator[Tensor]:
        for i in range(self.end):
            yield torch.tensor(i)
```

This section develops the concept step by step and uses the following code to make the behavior concrete.

```{python}
dataset = SimpleCountingDataset(end=10)
dataloader = utils.DataLoader(dataset, batch_size=4)

for i, batch in enumerate(dataloader, start=1):
    print(f'Batch {i}: {batch}')
```

The important point is the distinction between mathematical intent and the way PyTorch records or manages state.

::: {.list-table tbl-colwidths="[20,20,30,45]"}

:::

```{python}
class ShardedCountingDataset(utils.IterableDataset):
    def __init__(self, end: int):
        self.end = end

    def __iter__(self) -> Iterator[Tensor]:
        worker_info = utils.get_worker_info()

        if worker_info is None:
            start = 0
            step = 1
        else:
            start = worker_info.id
            step = worker_info.num_workers

        for i in range(start, self.end, step):
            yield torch.tensor(i)
```

In normal training code, this pattern keeps the model logic explicit while letting PyTorch handle the repetitive bookkeeping.

::: {.callout-tip}

:::

## 2.3.8 A DataLoader Configuration Closer to a Training Script

The example below is small on purpose: it exposes the mechanism without burying it inside a large model.

::: {.callout-note}

:::

```{python}
device = torch.accelerator.current_accelerator(check_available=True)
if device is None:
    device = torch.device('cpu')

dataset = utils.TensorDataset(X, y)
dataloader = utils.DataLoader(
    dataset,
    batch_size=64,
    shuffle=True,
    num_workers=2,
    pin_memory=device.type == 'cuda',
    persistent_workers=True,
)
```

These details are easy to overlook, but they explain many common PyTorch errors and performance surprises.

```python
for X, y in dataloader:
    X = X.to(device, non_blocking=True)
    y = y.to(device, non_blocking=True)

    y_pred = model(X)
    loss = loss_fn(y_pred, y)
    loss.backward()
    optimizer.step()
    optimizer.zero_grad()
```

This section develops the concept step by step and uses the following code to make the behavior concrete.

## 2.3.9 Summary

The important point is the distinction between mathematical intent and the way PyTorch records or manages state.
